title-group.article-title: IoT-Driven classroom air quality management with deep hierarchical cluster analysis

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/00qzypv28grid.412813.d0000 0001 0687 4946School of Computer Science and Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Chennai Campus, Chennai, India
2https://ror.org/01aams6440000 0004 1774 1876Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Easwari Engineering College, Chennai, India
3https://ror.org/0281pgk040000 0004 5937 9932Department of Information Technology, Velammal Engineering College, Chennai, India

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Poor classroom air quality and high levels of dust have a detrimental impact on student health, comfort, and productivity. To address this issue, we propose an IoT enabled system for monitoring and predicting classroom air quality and dust levels. The system employs multiple sensors placed inside the classroom to collect air quality data. Deep hierarchical cluster analysis is utilized to group the data at different levels of granularity, enabling the extraction of meaningful insights from large volumes of data and revealing trends and patterns that may be challenging to identify using conventional techniques. The work aims to comprehend the factors influencing classroom air quality and dust levels and develop an accurate prediction model. We leverage machine learning algorithms, specifically deep hierarchical cluster analysis, to uncover hidden patterns and relationships within the data. By applying deep hierarchical cluster analysis, we categorize the air quality and dust levels of a classroom into distinct clusters or groups based on data point similarity. Subsequently, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model is developed based on the cluster analysis results to predict air quality and dust levels. The results showed that the system is suitable for real-time implementation, indicating its potential to be used in practical applications.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Indoor pollution is caused by a variety of factors, including but not limited to, human exhalation, smoking, fuel burning, and the proximity of the indoor environment to surrounding outdoor sources of pollutants. Outdoor sources of pollution can include busy roads, highways, industrial regions, and other sources of industrial activity that emit harmful particulate matter and chemicals. These pollutants can accumulate in enclosed spaces, such as classrooms, and contribute to poor air quality, which can have significant health implications for occupants, particularly those with pre-existing respiratory conditions1.

Indoor pollution can have a significant impact on a person’s health by creating cardiovascular and respiratory ailments, as well as altering human cognition, because the average person spends roughly 80–90% of their time indoors2–4. Students spend a significant amount of their time in classrooms, ranging from 30 to 40% of their time. During this period, they are exposed to a variety of pollutants, including those mentioned above. In addition, the use of chalk in classrooms can contribute to dust particles in the air, which can further exacerbate the problem. Respiratory problems and various health concerns may arise, especially among students with pre-existing conditions like asthma, as a result of extended contact with airborne pollutants and dust particles5–7.

Thus, addressing indoor air pollution in classrooms is crucial for promoting a healthy learning environment for students. Such exposure can also impact on human cognition, causing fatigue and decreased performance. Maintaining a healthy indoor learning environment is essential for the well-being and academic performance of students. To achieve this, it is important to monitor and predict the indoor air quality regularly.

The presence of pollutants such as carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), ammonia (NH3), sulphur (S), benzene (C6H6), and particulate matter (PM) like PM2.5 and PM10 can significantly impact the air quality in classrooms. The concentration of these pollutants can be used to calculate the Air Quality Index (AQI). By regularly monitoring the AQI, it is possible to assess pollution levels and take appropriate actions to protect the health of students and faculty. An air quality monitoring system that employs AQI as a metric for assessing pollution levels can be highly beneficial in maintaining a healthy learning environment. Such a system can alert faculty and initiate immediate action in the event of extreme pollution levels, thereby ensuring the safety and well-being of everyone in the classroom. By continuously monitoring indoor air quality and generating alerts when necessary, this system can help prevent respiratory issues like asthma, allergies, and other health problems caused by prolonged exposure to polluted air.

This paper proposes an IoT based classroom air quality monitoring system that allows user to observe and monitor live air quality within the classroom using IoT. Sensors are used in the classroom to measure the air quality index and dust particles density. The sensed data is continuously transmitted to the server. Furthermore, sensor data is displayed on a webpage to determine the status of pollution in classroom. The data collected at 10-minute intervals is used to forecast air quality index (AQI) and dust density for a 1-hour time horizon, enabling proactive management of classroom air quality. This IoT based system offers a practical and cost-effective solution for monitoring and predicting classroom air quality, ensuring a safe and healthy learning environment for students and teachers alike.
Our key contributions are as follows:

list-item.p: Development of an IoT enabled system for monitoring and predicting classroom air quality and dust levels.

Application of deep hierarchical cluster analysis to categorize air quality data into Low, Moderate, and High Pollution groups, revealing insights such as a correlation between poor ventilation and elevated PM2.5/PM10 levels, diurnal dust density peaks during high chalk usage, and increased CO2/AQI with higher occupant density. These findings guide targeted interventions like enhanced ventilation and inform feature selection for the prediction model.
Development of an accurate prediction model based on LSTM, enhancing proactive management of classroom environments.

sec.p: The novelty of this work lies in several unique aspects that distinguish it from prior studies:

list-item.p: Comprehensive integration of AQI and dust density monitoring: The system simultaneously monitors a wide range of pollutants (CO, CO₂, NH₃, S, C₆H₆, PM₂.₅, PM₁₀) along with dust density, offering a more complete indoor air quality assessment than prior classroom-focused studies.

Cost-effective hardware design: By using low-cost sensors (MQ135 for gases, GP2Y1014AU0F for dust) and an Arduino UNO-based prototype, the system provides an affordable and scalable alternative to more expensive multi-sensor platforms.
Novel analytical approach: The combined use of deep hierarchical clustering and LSTM-based forecasting results in predictions with less error.

Real-time, classroom-specific scalability: The system supports continuous data transmission and web-based visualization tailored for educational settings, filling a gap in practical classroom air quality solutions.

sec.p: The remainder of the paper is arranged as follows: Sect. “Related works” focuses on recent work in the field of monitoring and forecasting classroom/indoor air quality. Section “Material and methods” describes the proposed system as well as its measurement and prediction capabilities. In Sect. “Results and discussion”, the proposed system’s outcomes are shown. Section “Conclusion and future work” concludes with a summary of the findings and recommendations for future research.

sec.title: Related works
sec.p: This section highlights the overview of existing works in the domain of IoT enabled classroom and indoor air quality, dust density monitoring and forecasting system.

sec.title: Indoor air quality monitoring and prediction system
sec.p: 8proposes an air quality management system that combines the indoor air quality index and humidity index into an improved indoor air quality index using real-time sensor data. A network of sensors, including a humidity sensor, detects indoor air pollution levels using an extended fractional-order Kalman filter (EFKF). Timely air quality alerts are delivered to enhance prediction accuracy and performance in dealing with measurement noise and nonlinearity. The use of expensive multi-sensors to measure many parameters raises the system’s cost in real-time implementation.

Toluene, xylene, and formaldehyde are volatile organic compounds (VOCs) that can adversely affect human health and present challenges for indoor air quality (IAQ) management9. In their study, the authors in9 propose a comprehensive control technique, the fuzzy genetic multi-layer control scheme (FGMLCS), to regulate IAQ. This multilayer control structure integrates fuzzy logic, evolutionary algorithms, and multi-objective optimization to achieve optimal control and enhance IAQ.

10proposed an Internet of Things (IoT) device that forecasts atmospheric pollution levels using the autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) method. Real-time and accurate measurements are provided by this system, which has a substantial positive impact on environmental safety and public health.

In11, a DBU-LSTM neural network model is introduced, which effectively captures temporal and spatial correlations in time series data bidirectionally. This model utilizes diverse data sources like points of interest (POI) and road networks to evaluate similarities among metropolitan regions. To address incomplete air quality data from previous monitoring stations, a tensor decomposition technique is employed. However, it is worth noting that the model’s drawback lies in its less accurate predictions and high computational power requirements.

In12, the authors proposed an indoor air quality monitoring and forecasting system based on an IoT and discrete-time Markov chain13. PM10, PM2.5, and CO levels are used as features for forecasting within the Markov chain. However, the accuracy of both measured and forecasted values needs improvement.

sec.title: Classroom air quality monitoring and prediction system
sec.p: The work in14 presents a monitoring and prediction system for air quality in educational institutions. Environmental data such as CO2 concentration, relative humidity, and temperature are collected through sensors in an indoor laboratory setting. The researchers utilized an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to estimate CO2 levels, achieving a prediction accuracy of approximately 10.6%.

CO2 and PM2.5 are detected by using low-cost sensors1. Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and eXtream Gradient Boosting Regression (XGBR) are used to estimate the air quality in rooms that don’t have sensors in real-time. Moreover, a modified Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model is used to forecast the air quality of rooms, which has an estimation accuracy of around 95% and a forecasting accuracy of around 96%.

CO2, PM10, and PM2.5 levels have been measured and predicted in a school environment15. The prediction algorithm is based on a multi-objective genetic algorithm that avoids false regression by excluding irrelevant input variables from prediction. PM2.5 levels in school environments have been measured and predicted16. The use of a 7-class regression model to predict PM2.5 levels has an accuracy of 80%.

While the existing literature provides valuable insights into IoT-based indoor air quality (IAQ) monitoring and forecasting systems, several research gaps remain. Many studies, such as those by8,9, rely on complex and costly multi-sensor setups or advanced computational models like FGMLCS, which may not be feasible for widespread deployment in resource-constrained environments like classrooms. Additionally, models like the DBU-LSTM proposed by11 suffer from high computational demands and less accurate predictions, limiting their practicality for real-time applications. Furthermore, existing classroom-specific studies, such as those by1,14.

The work in11, often focus on limited pollutants (e.g., CO₂ or PM₂.₅) and lack comprehensive integration of dust density monitoring alongside air quality index (AQI) forecasting. The accuracy of forecasting in some studies, such as in12, also requires improvement to ensure reliable proactive measures.

To address these gaps, this paper proposes a cost-effective, IoT-based classroom air quality monitoring system that integrates both AQI and dust density measurements. By employing a combination of hierarchical clustering and LSTM-based prediction models, our approach achieves high accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment. This system provides a practical solution for continuous monitoring and forecasting, enabling timely interventions to ensure a healthy learning environment.

sec.title: Description of classrooms
sec.p: We select a classroom from the Computer Science block of the institution for our experiment. A brief description of classroom specification is mentioned in Table 1.

sec.title: Data acquisition
sec.p: Data collection is the most important step for evaluating our proposed system, and it is achieved by creating an IoT-based device to track indoor dust and the air quality index. The developed device is cost-effective, portable and easy to use. Additionally, a web application has been developed for collection data from the classroom and the data has been stored in the cloud. The block diagram of the proposed system is shown in Fig. 1.

caption.p: Block diagram of the proposed system.

sec.p: A prototype of the developed device is shown in Fig. 2. The prototype is placed near the front of the classroom board. The readings are taken between 9.00 am and 5.00 pm. The information is gathered over the course of 25 days.

caption.p: Hardware prototype of the proposed system.

sec.p: The Fig. 2 shows the prototype of the proposed classroom air quality monitoring and prediction system.

The Arduino UNO17 is a device that collects and processes sensor data. There are six analog pins and fourteen digital pins on the device. The Arduino UNO is a low cost microcontroller that uses the ATmega328 and operates on 8-bit data. The sensor data is wirelessly transmitted to the webserver via the ESP8266 transceiver18.

When the target polluting gas is present, the sensor’s conductivity increases proportionally to the gas concentration. Ammonia, sulphur, and benzene, as well as smoke and other hazardous gases, are detected by the MQ135 gas sensor19. It is a low-cost sensor that can detect a wide range of hazardous gases and has a wide range of applications.

The dust sensor GP2Y1010AU0F20 works based on optical sensing. GP2Y1010AU0F is very good at detecting very small particles. The dust sensor uses very little power ranging from 11 mA to 20 mA and can be powered directly from the Arduino board. The output of the sensor is an analog voltage proportional to the observed dust density.

sec.title: Data pre-processing
sec.p: The pre-processing procedures aid in decreasing the noise in the data, which ultimately speeds up processing and improves generalization ability. To obtain the values between two successive sample points, we employed interpolation21. The system is smoothed out to default.

sec.title: Hierarchical clustering analysis
sec.p: The Algorithm 1 represents the working of hierarchical clustering algorithm. The algorithm begins with a dataset of n data points. A dissimilarity matrix D is computed, measuring the dissimilarity between each pair of data points using the euclidean distance22.
for k = 1 to p, where x[i, k] and x[j, k] are the values of the kth feature of data points i and j, respectively.

Initially, each data point is assigned to an individual cluster. The algorithm iteratively merges the two closest clusters based on the dissimilarity matrix. The linkage criterion determines how the distance between clusters is calculated, with options such as single linkage, complete linkage, or average linkage. The dissimilarity matrix is updated by recalculating the dissimilarities between the new cluster and the remaining clusters. Merged clusters are removed from the list. The algorithm continues until only one cluster remains, representing the complete hierarchy. The output is a dendrogram, a visual representation of the clustering process. The dendrogram displays the hierarchy of clusters and the order in which they were merged. The height at which clusters are merged indicates the dissimilarity or distance at which the merge occurred.
The air quality samples are partitioned into distinct subsets using hierarchical cluster analysis, which is performed with the aim of achieving the objective of indoor air quality monitoring.

This group consists of indoor spaces with low levels of pollutants such as particulate matter (PM), Air quality index. These spaces have good ventilation systems, effective air filters, and low occupant density. The air quality in this group is considered to be of high quality.

This group consists of indoor spaces with moderate levels of pollutants. The levels of PM and Air quality index may be slightly higher compared to Group 1. These spaces may have average ventilation systems and moderate occupant density. The air quality in this group is considered to be moderate.

This group consists of indoor spaces with high levels of pollutants. The levels of PM and Air quality index are significantly higher compared to Group 1 and Group 2. These spaces may have poor ventilation systems, lack of air filters, or high occupant density. The air quality in this group is considered to be poor.

sec.title: Prediction
sec.p: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are computational models inspired by the structure and function of the human brain. They consist of interconnected nodes called neurons, organized in layers. ANNs are capable of learning complex patterns and relationships in data through a training process. LSTM23 is a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) that addresses the vanishing gradient problem, which is common in traditional RNNs. LSTM networks are well-suited for handling time-series data as they can capture long-term dependencies and store information over extended sequences.
To construct an ANN model with LSTM layers for time-series prediction, the following steps are involved24 as shown in Fig. 3:

list-item.label: i.
list-item.p: Architecture Design: Determine the number and arrangement of LSTM layers in the network. Each LSTM layer will have a specific number of memory cells or hidden units that can retain information from previous time steps.

list-item.label: ii.
list-item.p: Input Preparation: Prepare the input data in a suitable format for the LSTM network. In the case of time-series prediction, the input data should include the historical values of the features (e.g., AQI and dust level) at different time steps.

list-item.label: iii.
list-item.p: LSTM Layer Configuration: Configure each LSTM layer by specifying the number of memory cells or hidden units within the layer.

list-item.label: iv.
list-item.p: Connection and Data Flow: Connect the LSTM layers sequentially to form the network. The output of one LSTM layer serves as the input to the next layer until the final layer is reached.

list-item.label: v.
list-item.p: Training: Train the ANN model with LSTM layers using a suitable optimization algorithm, such as backpropagation. During training, the model learns to adjust the weights and biases of the LSTM cells to minimize the prediction error between the actual and predicted values.

list-item.label: vi.
list-item.p: Time-Series Prediction: Once the model is trained, it can be used for time-series prediction. Provide the current and previous values of the features (e.g., AQI and dust level) as input to the LSTM layers, and the model will generate predictions for future time steps.

sec.p: The equation computes LSTM are as follows:

where h(t) represents the hidden layer state, x(t) represents the input data (including AQI and dust level), y(t) represents the predicted output, and the variables (U, V, W) represent the model’s weights. The biases are denoted by the new variables (V(i), V(f), V(o), V(c)).

sec.title: Results and discussion
sec.p: This section describes the results obtained by our proposed system.

caption.p: Air quality level display on the webpage.

sec.p: The Fig. 4 shows the value of air quality measured by MQ135 sensor and dust density is measured by optical dust sensor are displayed on the webpage. In our observation we found the air quality falls in the normal range and dust value ranges from normal to moderate. According to air quality index25, the moderate AQI value ranges up to 0–150 ppm and the moderate Dust density26 ranges up to 0–50 µg/m3. During the days of observation, the values of air quality and dust density were found to be in the normal to satisfactory range. The reason for this is that there is better ventilation in the classroom, better air flow, and more empty space around the classroom.

To assess the predictive capability of the proposed model for classroom air quality and dust density, a comparative statistical error analysis is conducted against two benchmark neural network architectures: LSTM and RNN27. These models are selected for their proven effectiveness in time-series forecasting, particularly in capturing temporal dependencies in environmental data. The Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), which quantifies the square root of the mean squared differences between predicted and observed values, serves as the primary evaluation metric. The summarized results are presented in Table 2, and the corresponding RMSE distributions are illustrated in Figure. 5. The LSTM model yields RMSE values of 0.1500 for air quality index (AQI) and 0.3000 for dust density. The RNN model performs slightly better, with RMSE values of 0.1400 for AQI and 0.2800 for dust density. The proposed model, which integrates hierarchical clustering with LSTM, achieves the best performance, recording RMSE values of 0.1300 for AQI and 0.2515 for dust density. These results demonstrate the superior predictive accuracy of the proposed model for classroom air quality and dust concentration levels.

To evaluate the predictive performance of the models, a statistical error analysis is conducted, with the summarized results presented in Table 2. The corresponding RMSE distributions for the LSTM, RNN, and the proposed model are illustrated in Fig. 5. RMSE, representing the square root of the average squared differences between the predicted and observed values, serves as a suitable metric for assessing regression-based prediction models. The LSTM model yielded RMSE values of 0.15 for AQI and 0.3000 for dust density. The RNN model showed a slight improvement, with RMSE values of 0.1400 and 0.2800 for AQI and dust density, respectively. The proposed model, integrating hierarchical clustering with LSTM, achieved the lowest RMSE values among the three, recording 0.1300 for AQI and 0.2515 for dust density. These findings indicate that the proposed model offers enhanced predictive accuracy for classroom air quality and dust concentration levels.

caption.p: Performance analysis of proposed model in terms of average MAE/MSE/RMSE.

sec.p: The Table 2 shows the performance analysis of the proposed method on the classification of different pollutants. The proposed model has achieved maximum performance with minimum error.

Figure 6 shows the measured and predicted air quality over a specific time period. With an error rate of 0.2 to 0.6, the proposed model is suitable for measuring air quality in a classroom during the day.

Figure 7 shows the measured and predicted values of dust density over a specific time period. The results show that the error rate ranges from 4 to 10 (µg/m3). During certain hours, the frequency of use of chalk is high, and construction work near the classroom’s perimeter causes the dust density prediction to differ from the predicted value.

sec.title: Conclusion and future work
sec.p: This paper presented and evaluated an IoT-based system for measuring and forecasting air quality and dust levels within a classroom environment. The prototype was deployed and tested on a small scale, demonstrating that the system’s predictive capabilities can support timely precautionary measures when air quality or dust levels approach hazardous thresholds. The results indicate that the applied machine learning algorithms are suitable for real-time implementation and practical use.

For future work, we plan to expand the deployment to cover a larger network of classrooms and to further assess the scalability and robustness of the proposed algorithm. Additionally, we intend to extend data collection across all seasons and under a wider range of environmental conditions. This will enable us to validate and enhance the system’s performance, ensuring its generalizability and reliability for continuous classroom air quality monitoring and prediction throughout the year.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Data availability The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
